lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> The interview opens with formal disclaimers and acknowledgments, setting the stage for an oral history project supported by the Idaho Historical Society and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Joyce Green introduces herself as a member of the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary history committee and outlines the goals of the interview: to explore the origins of the Society and Marcia Davies' personal journey in art. The context and significance of the interview are established, emphasizing its role in commemorating Idaho's artistic legacy.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The interview opens with formal disclaimers and acknowledgments, setting the stage for an oral history project supported by the Idaho Historical Society and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Joyce Green introduces herself as a member of the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary history committee and outlines the goals of the interview: to explore the origins of the Society and Marcia Davies' personal journey in art. The context and significance of the interview are established, emphasizing its role in commemorating Idaho's artistic legacy.

lines.line: # Early Life, Family, and Move to Idaho

> Marcia Davies shares her family background, including the loss of her mother in a car accident and her upbringing with relatives in Arkansas. She describes her family's geographic dispersion and her educational beginnings at the University of Arkansas at Little Rock. Marcia recounts her move to Boise, Idaho in 1976, prompted by her first husband's job, and her initial impressions of the city during a drought year. She also discusses her decision to continue her education at Boise State University after establishing residency.

metadata.title: Early Life, Family, and Move to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Marcia Davies shares her family background, including the loss of her mother in a car accident and her upbringing with relatives in Arkansas. She describes her family's geographic dispersion and her educational beginnings at the University of Arkansas at Little Rock. Marcia recounts her move to Boise, Idaho in 1976, prompted by her first husband's job, and her initial impressions of the city during a drought year. She also discusses her decision to continue her education at Boise State University after establishing residency.

lines.line: # College Experience and Introduction to Watercolor

> Marcia reflects on her time at Boise State University, where she immersed herself in upper-division art classes after transferring from Arkansas. She describes the variety of courses she took, including pottery, sculpture, printmaking, oil painting, and life drawing, and credits her exposure to watercolors to her time working in an Atlanta gift shop. Influenced by artists like Hubert Shuptrine and Andrew Wyeth, Marcia developed a deep appreciation for watercolor, which would shape her artistic path.

metadata.title: College Experience and Introduction to Watercolor
metadata.synopsis: Marcia reflects on her time at Boise State University, where she immersed herself in upper-division art classes after transferring from Arkansas. She describes the variety of courses she took, including pottery, sculpture, printmaking, oil painting, and life drawing, and credits her exposure to watercolors to her time working in an Atlanta gift shop. Influenced by artists like Hubert Shuptrine and Andrew Wyeth, Marcia developed a deep appreciation for watercolor, which would shape her artistic path.

lines.line: # Attraction to Watercolor as a Medium

> Marcia discusses her fascination with watercolor, inspired by the works of Hubert Shuptrine. She describes the medium's unique qualities—its fluidity, transparency, and versatility—and admits she was initially unaware of its reputation as a challenging medium. Marcia appreciates watercolor's portability and the forgiving nature compared to oils, sharing anecdotes about her early experiences and the practical advantages that drew her to the medium.

metadata.title: Attraction to Watercolor as a Medium
metadata.synopsis: Marcia discusses her fascination with watercolor, inspired by the works of Hubert Shuptrine. She describes the medium's unique qualities—its fluidity, transparency, and versatility—and admits she was initially unaware of its reputation as a challenging medium. Marcia appreciates watercolor's portability and the forgiving nature compared to oils, sharing anecdotes about her early experiences and the practical advantages that drew her to the medium.

lines.line: # Joining and Early Involvement in the Idaho Watercolor Society

> Marcia recounts how she first learned about the Idaho Watercolor Society during her final year at Boise State, joining as a charter member. She recalls early meetings, the Society's first open show, and her participation as an exhibitor. Marcia tries to remember other founding members and reflects on the collaborative spirit of the group's beginnings, highlighting the informal and inclusive atmosphere that characterized the early days.

metadata.title: Joining and Early Involvement in the Idaho Watercolor Society
metadata.synopsis: Marcia recounts how she first learned about the Idaho Watercolor Society during her final year at Boise State, joining as a charter member. She recalls early meetings, the Society's first open show, and her participation as an exhibitor. Marcia tries to remember other founding members and reflects on the collaborative spirit of the group's beginnings, highlighting the informal and inclusive atmosphere that characterized the early days.

lines.line: # Early Career and Intersection with Art Education

> Marcia describes her initial foray into the workforce as a file clerk at an insurance company, noting that her art education, particularly life drawing, unexpectedly benefited her in understanding medical reports. She explains how her knowledge of anatomy from art classes gave her an edge in claims handling, demonstrating the practical value of her artistic background in a seemingly unrelated field. Marcia emphasizes the importance of embracing all learning experiences, as they can have unforeseen applications.

metadata.title: Early Career and Intersection with Art Education
metadata.synopsis: Marcia describes her initial foray into the workforce as a file clerk at an insurance company, noting that her art education, particularly life drawing, unexpectedly benefited her in understanding medical reports. She explains how her knowledge of anatomy from art classes gave her an edge in claims handling, demonstrating the practical value of her artistic background in a seemingly unrelated field. Marcia emphasizes the importance of embracing all learning experiences, as they can have unforeseen applications.

lines.line: # Career Progression and Roles in Insurance and Safety

> Marcia outlines her career progression from clerical work to claims handling, investigation, and eventually safety and risk management. She describes her enjoyment of investigative work, her transition to training and consulting with employers, and her eventual leadership role at the State Insurance Fund. Marcia reflects on her long tenure in the field, the variety of responsibilities she held, and her retirement in 2016.

metadata.title: Career Progression and Roles in Insurance and Safety
metadata.synopsis: Marcia outlines her career progression from clerical work to claims handling, investigation, and eventually safety and risk management. She describes her enjoyment of investigative work, her transition to training and consulting with employers, and her eventual leadership role at the State Insurance Fund. Marcia reflects on her long tenure in the field, the variety of responsibilities she held, and her retirement in 2016.

lines.line: # Early Roles and Responsibilities in the Watercolor Society

> Marcia details her early involvement in the Idaho Watercolor Society, including producing the first newsletter on an electric typewriter and later contributing regular columns. She describes her role as a regional representative, organizing local activities such as potlucks, demonstrations, and workshops. Marcia highlights the collaborative nature of the Society's board meetings and the importance of fostering both social and educational opportunities for members.

metadata.title: Early Roles and Responsibilities in the Watercolor Society
metadata.synopsis: Marcia details her early involvement in the Idaho Watercolor Society, including producing the first newsletter on an electric typewriter and later contributing regular columns. She describes her role as a regional representative, organizing local activities such as potlucks, demonstrations, and workshops. Marcia highlights the collaborative nature of the Society's board meetings and the importance of fostering both social and educational opportunities for members.

lines.line: # Regional Activities, Early Shows, and Rotunda Exhibit

> The conversation explores the structure of the Society's regional divisions, the expectations for local activities, and the development of open and juried shows. Marcia and Joyce discuss the origins of the Rotunda show at the Idaho State Capitol, the logistics of organizing exhibits, and the evolution of the Society's exhibition practices. They reflect on the challenges and successes of engaging members across Idaho's diverse regions.

metadata.title: Regional Activities, Early Shows, and Rotunda Exhibit
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the structure of the Society's regional divisions, the expectations for local activities, and the development of open and juried shows. Marcia and Joyce discuss the origins of the Rotunda show at the Idaho State Capitol, the logistics of organizing exhibits, and the evolution of the Society's exhibition practices. They reflect on the challenges and successes of engaging members across Idaho's diverse regions.

lines.line: # Personal Artistic Growth and Approach to Watercolor

> Marcia shares insights into her artistic process, noting that she achieves better results when she paints loosely and reacts to the medium rather than trying to control it. She discusses the importance of enjoying the creative process, knowing when to stop, and the value of stepping back from a painting. Marcia reflects on the lessons learned from teachers and the challenges of finishing work in workshop settings.

metadata.title: Personal Artistic Growth and Approach to Watercolor
metadata.synopsis: Marcia shares insights into her artistic process, noting that she achieves better results when she paints loosely and reacts to the medium rather than trying to control it. She discusses the importance of enjoying the creative process, knowing when to stop, and the value of stepping back from a painting. Marcia reflects on the lessons learned from teachers and the challenges of finishing work in workshop settings.

lines.line: # Workshops, Local Artists, and Experimentation

> Marcia recalls attending workshops led by prominent jurors and local artists, including Jean Dobie, Bill Benson, and Gay Hoops. She describes the variety of techniques and styles she encountered, from traditional washes to experimental approaches using ink, tissue paper, and plastic wrap. Marcia highlights the value of learning from different instructors and the inspiration she gained from both structured and unconventional workshops.

metadata.title: Workshops, Local Artists, and Experimentation
metadata.synopsis: Marcia recalls attending workshops led by prominent jurors and local artists, including Jean Dobie, Bill Benson, and Gay Hoops. She describes the variety of techniques and styles she encountered, from traditional washes to experimental approaches using ink, tissue paper, and plastic wrap. Marcia highlights the value of learning from different instructors and the inspiration she gained from both structured and unconventional workshops.

lines.line: # Collecting Local Art and Supporting Artists

> Marcia discusses her passion for collecting artwork by Idaho artists, often rescuing pieces from thrift and consignment stores. She describes her growing collection, which includes works by Richard Byrd, Hugh Mosman, Dwight Williams, and others, as well as her efforts to find homes for these pieces in places like a backcountry lodge. Marcia expresses pride in supporting local artists and cherishes the connections she has made through her collection.

metadata.title: Collecting Local Art and Supporting Artists
metadata.synopsis: Marcia discusses her passion for collecting artwork by Idaho artists, often rescuing pieces from thrift and consignment stores. She describes her growing collection, which includes works by Richard Byrd, Hugh Mosman, Dwight Williams, and others, as well as her efforts to find homes for these pieces in places like a backcountry lodge. Marcia expresses pride in supporting local artists and cherishes the connections she has made through her collection.

lines.line: # Presidency and Organizational Contributions

> Marcia recounts her tenure as president of the Idaho Watercolor Society, beginning in 1989, and the challenges of recruiting volunteers for leadership roles. She describes her initiative to acquire new, versatile display panels for the Society's exhibits, replacing the heavy wooden ones her husband had built. Marcia expresses pride in this lasting contribution and reflects on the ongoing need for volunteerism in arts organizations.

metadata.title: Presidency and Organizational Contributions
metadata.synopsis: Marcia recounts her tenure as president of the Idaho Watercolor Society, beginning in 1989, and the challenges of recruiting volunteers for leadership roles. She describes her initiative to acquire new, versatile display panels for the Society's exhibits, replacing the heavy wooden ones her husband had built. Marcia expresses pride in this lasting contribution and reflects on the ongoing need for volunteerism in arts organizations.

lines.line: # Stepping Back, Toastmasters, and Public Speaking

> Marcia explains her decision to step back from the Watercolor Society as her career and involvement in Toastmasters intensified. She describes the support she received from her employer to participate in Toastmasters, her achievements as a Distinguished Toastmaster, and her enjoyment of public speaking and leadership. Marcia notes her eventual return to the Society and her plans to participate in upcoming exhibits.

metadata.title: Stepping Back, Toastmasters, and Public Speaking
metadata.synopsis: Marcia explains her decision to step back from the Watercolor Society as her career and involvement in Toastmasters intensified. She describes the support she received from her employer to participate in Toastmasters, her achievements as a Distinguished Toastmaster, and her enjoyment of public speaking and leadership. Marcia notes her eventual return to the Society and her plans to participate in upcoming exhibits.

lines.line: # Integration of Art and Professional Life

> Marcia describes how her artistic background influenced her professional work in safety and risk management. She shares the story of presenting a paper titled 'Right Brain Safety' at the World Congress on Safety and Health, emphasizing her practical, creative approach to workplace safety. Marcia highlights the value of integrating right-brain thinking into traditionally left-brain fields and the recognition she received for her innovative perspective.

metadata.title: Integration of Art and Professional Life
metadata.synopsis: Marcia describes how her artistic background influenced her professional work in safety and risk management. She shares the story of presenting a paper titled 'Right Brain Safety' at the World Congress on Safety and Health, emphasizing her practical, creative approach to workplace safety. Marcia highlights the value of integrating right-brain thinking into traditionally left-brain fields and the recognition she received for her innovative perspective.

lines.line: # Reflections on the Watercolor Society Community

> Marcia reflects on the welcoming and supportive culture of the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting its inclusivity and the positive atmosphere at critiques and meetings. She appreciates the connections members have made with national organizations and the encouragement offered to artists of all levels. Marcia draws parallels between the Society's approach to critique and the supportive feedback culture in Toastmasters, emphasizing the importance of community in artistic growth.

metadata.title: Reflections on the Watercolor Society Community
metadata.synopsis: Marcia reflects on the welcoming and supportive culture of the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting its inclusivity and the positive atmosphere at critiques and meetings. She appreciates the connections members have made with national organizations and the encouragement offered to artists of all levels. Marcia draws parallels between the Society's approach to critique and the supportive feedback culture in Toastmasters, emphasizing the importance of community in artistic growth.

lines.line: # Founding Members and Legacy of the Society

> Marcia shares an anecdote about Dr. Slaughter, the son of founding member Ruth Clark, and reflects on Clark's pivotal role in establishing the Idaho Watercolor Society. She acknowledges Clark's influence in connecting the Society with the Boise Art Museum and other organizations, as well as her broader contributions to the arts community. Marcia expresses gratitude for visionary leaders and the importance of both leadership and participation in sustaining the Society.

metadata.title: Founding Members and Legacy of the Society
metadata.synopsis: Marcia shares an anecdote about Dr. Slaughter, the son of founding member Ruth Clark, and reflects on Clark's pivotal role in establishing the Idaho Watercolor Society. She acknowledges Clark's influence in connecting the Society with the Boise Art Museum and other organizations, as well as her broader contributions to the arts community. Marcia expresses gratitude for visionary leaders and the importance of both leadership and participation in sustaining the Society.

lines.line: # Closing Thoughts on Art and Education

> The interview concludes with a toast to the arts and a discussion of the importance of art in education and daily life. Marcia and Joyce express their appreciation for hands-on artistic experiences and reference Winston Churchill's views on the value of art. They celebrate the Idaho Watercolor Society's milestone anniversary and express hope for the continued vitality of the arts in Idaho and beyond.

story.title: Marsha Davies_1-28-2026
story.description: The interview is with Joyce Green, a member of the 50th Anniversary History Committee of the Idaho Watercolor Society, and Marsha Davies, a key early figure in the Society's history. The conversation covers Marsha's personal background, her journey to Idaho, and her involvement with the Idaho Watercolor Society from its inception in 1976. Topics include the origins and evolution of the Society, her contributions such as early newsletters, and Society activities. Marsha shares insights into her career in insurance and safety, her love for watercolor painting, and her prominent role in Toastmasters. The interview emphasizes the supportive community within the Society and its dedication to promoting arts in Idaho.
